one_line_takeaway: By explicitly training black-box DNN watermarks to reject Gaussian-noise-perturbed 'approximate keys' near the original trigger (via a specificity metric derived from an adaptively-optimized noise upper bound), SEW shrinks the reverse-engineerable key space by roughly 10^108x on CIFAR-10-VGG16 and lifts watermark accuracy under Dehydra/MOTH/FeatureRE removal attacks from single-to-double digits (for most baselines) to ~97-99%, at the cost of increased fragility to direct noise-based perturbation of the key itself.
key_finding: On CIFAR-10-VGG16, SEW reduces Spec from 0.3569 (SEW-Pre, i.e., before specificity enhancement) to 0.0364 (SEW-Post) while CDA stays at 92.79% (vs. 93.16% clean model) and WACC remains 100%; the paper computes that this Spec reduction corresponds to an approximately 6x10^-108 shrinkage in the hypersphere volume of the approximate-key space (~10^108 orders of magnitude fewer potential approximate keys), using key dimensionality n=6x6x3=108 from the patch size. Under three unlearning-based removal attacks on CIFAR-10 (Table 3), SEW-Post retains WACC of 98.67% (Dehydra), 97.67% (MOTH), and 98.33% (FeatureRE) with CDA 90.7-92.2%, versus most baselines collapsing to single-digit-to-tens WACC under Dehydra (e.g., Content 4.0%, Noise 3.67%, ISSBA 4.33%) — though EW (88.67% under Dehydra) and WES (93.0% under Dehydra) also showed some resistance, attributed partly to non-specificity mechanisms (EW's exponential weighting, ADI/MW's multi-label dispersion). Neural Cleanse reverse-engineered keys from SEW-Post models were visually 'nearly indistinguishable' from clean-model outputs (Figure 5), unlike baseline watermarked models which produced recognizable approximate triggers. Under fine-tuning/fine-pruning, WACC stayed above CDA until parameter changes were aggressive enough to already unacceptably degrade CDA. A causal explanation (RQ3) links robustness to comparing watermark-key specificity against the model's own clean-sample specificity: SEW-Pre's watermark specificity (0.3569) exceeded the model's baseline specificity (0.0668), enabling reverse engineering, while SEW-Post's (0.0364) fell below the model's clean specificity (0.0653), which the paper argues explains resistance. An ablation (Table 4, described in text) shows fixed sigma=0.01 yields poor WACC (53.0%), fixed sigma=1.0 yields weak Spec (0.2134), while the adaptive sigma scheme achieves both perfect WACC and lowest Spec. A perturbation-resistance test shows a fundamental trade-off: raising injected noise std from 0 to 0.05 drops CDA/WACC from 92.97%/100.00% to 62.56%/15.00%.
core_contribution: The paper introduces 'watermark specificity' — the precision with which a black-box DNN watermark responds only to its original key rather than to nearby 'approximate keys' — as a previously unexamined property that governs robustness to watermark-removal attacks. It (1) defines a quantitative Spec metric based on the noise upper bound (the largest Gaussian-noise norm an approximate key can tolerate while still triggering the watermark, estimated via an iterative gradient-based optimization over sigma), (2) shows existing black-box watermarking schemes have poor specificity, and (3) proposes Specificity-Enhanced Watermarking (SEW), which during embedding trains on three sample sets: clean samples (true labels), key samples (original key mapped to target label), and cover samples (approximate keys generated by adding calibrated Gaussian noise to the key, retaining true labels) to explicitly suppress the model's response to approximate keys while preserving response to the exact key.
threat_model: Explicitly stated in Section 3.1: an adversary A downloads an open-source watermarked model f_w (e.g., from HuggingFace) and wants to violate its OpenRAIL-M license by misusing it (openly or as a closed-source derivative) while evading IP tracing. A has white-box access to f_w's parameters (consistent with obtaining an open-source model) and knows a watermark exists but not the key. A's goal is to derive a surrogate model f_a that no longer responds to the watermark key. The paper restricts its evaluation to model-modification attacks (white-box: fine-tuning, pruning, unlearning-based reverse-engineering of approximate keys) and explicitly excludes model-extraction attacks, arguing (Section 3.2) that black-box extraction imposes prohibitive query/data/training costs relative to its lower access requirements, making it a less realistic threat under the stated open-source redistribution scenario. The defender's goal is watermark survival in f_a for later black-box ownership verification.
limitations: The paper's own text acknowledges: (1) an explicit specificity/perturbation-resistance trade-off (RQ4) -- SEW's watermark becomes fragile to input noise as specificity rises, and CDA/WACC both degrade sharply under sufficient perturbation, meaning a determined adversary using pure noise-based evasion (as opposed to reverse-engineering) can still degrade verification, albeit at real cost; (2) model-extraction attacks are excluded from evaluation by scope decision (argued as low-realism/high-cost under the stated threat model, not tested empirically); (3) the paper concedes other mechanisms besides specificity also contribute to robustness (EW's exponential weighting, ADI/MW's multi-label key dispersion), so specificity is presented as a strong but not sole explanatory factor; (4) results for CIFAR-100 and TinyImageNet removal-attack robustness are deferred to an appendix not captured in the excerpted text; (5) NLP generalization (SST-2/AGNews with BERT/Text-CNN) and STRIP-detection evasion are mentioned only briefly as a discussion point pointing to an appendix, without headline numbers in the main text extracted here; (6) MW baseline shows unusually low CDA on TinyImageNet (19.39%), suggesting some baselines were harder to reproduce faithfully or degrade severely on harder datasets, a caveat for cross-baseline comparability.

quantitative_results: CIFAR-10-VGG16: SEW-Post CDA 92.79%, WACC 100%, Spec 0.0364, vs SEW-Pre Spec 0.3569 and Clean Model CDA 93.16%
CIFAR-100-ResNet18: SEW-Post CDA 75.34%, WACC 100%, Spec 0.0342, vs SEW-Pre Spec 0.3372 and Clean Model CDA 75.87%
TinyImageNet-EN-B3: SEW-Post CDA 53.57%, WACC 100%, Spec 0.0381, vs SEW-Pre Spec 0.1354 and Clean Model CDA 53.90%
Approximate-key space volume reduction estimate for CIFAR-10-VGG16: ~6x10^-108 (i.e. ~10^108 fewer approximate keys), computed via n-dimensional hypersphere volume ratio with n=108
Removal-attack WACC on CIFAR-10 (Table 3): SEW-Post 98.67%±1.25 (Dehydra), 97.67%±0.94 (MOTH), 98.33%±0.47 (FeatureRE); SEW-Pre 2.67%±2.36 (Dehydra), 7.67%±6.8 (MOTH), 8.0%±7.87 (FeatureRE)
Comparative model specificity finding (RQ3): SEW-Pre watermark specificity 0.3569 vs model (clean-sample) specificity 0.0668; SEW-Post watermark specificity 0.0364 vs model specificity 0.0653
Perturbation-resistance test: noise std 0 -> 0.05 causes CDA/WACC to drop from 92.97%/100.00% to 62.56%/15.00%
Sigma-ablation (Table 4, CIFAR10-VGG16): fixed sigma=0.01 gives WACC 53.0%; fixed sigma=1.0 gives Spec 0.2134; adaptive sigma achieves perfect WACC with lowest Spec
MW baseline notably low CDA on TinyImageNet-EN-B3: 19.39% (vs Clean Model 53.90%)
baselines_compared: SEW-Pre (ablated variant of SEW before specificity enhancement)
